preference pairs under both harmful and clean instructions, fur-

ther mitigating the trade-off between safety and sample quality.
2
: Additionally, we propose a Dynamic Focusing Mechanism (DFM)
v  for SC-DPO, promoting the model’s learning of difficult preference
i
pair samples. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SC-DPO out-
X
r performs existing methods, effectively defending against various
a NSFW content while maintaining optimal sample quality and hu-
man preference alignment. Additionally, SC-DPO exhibits resilience
against adversarial prompts designed to generate harmful content.

Keywords
Text-to-Image Generation, NSFW Content Mitigation, Safety

-to-Image Generation via

Recent advances in text-to-image (T2I) generation have facilitated
the creation of high-quality images that faithfully follow the human
instructions [30, 32, 35, 37]. While these developments showcase
the immense capabilities of T2I models, their open-ended nature
also introduces significant challenges in the ethical and societal
implications of the generated content [36, 52]. Malicious users, for
example, can easily exploit T2I models to generate harmful images
with sexual, violent or biased representations [11, 48, 52], posing
risks to vulnerable audiences, especially minors. This growing con-
cern has prompted the research community to place increasing
emphasis on the safety of T2I model’s outputs [10, 15, 25, 34], with
a primary focus on preventing the generation of inappropriate con-
tent such as various NSFW (Not Safe For Work) concepts [20, 28, 51],
which can arise from the misuse of this powerful technology.
∗These authors contributed equally to this research.
†Corresponding authors.

2.1  Text-to-Image (T2I) Generation
Text-to-image (T2I) generation poses a long-stand topic in com-
puter vision. Early efforts primarily focused on generative adver-
sarial networks (GANs) [8, 13, 54]. In recent years, diffusion models
have emerged as a promising alternative, significantly improving
the quality and diversity of generated images. The DDPM [18]
laid the foundation for this paradigm by learning noise at various
steps and gradually recovering the target data distribution through
a denoising process. The introduction of guided diffusion mod-
els [6] further advanced the field by incorporating guidance mecha-
nisms (e.g., classifier-free guidance) to align generated images with
specific conditions, such as text descriptions. Furthermore, recent
commercial models, such as Imagen [37], DALL·E [32, 33], and

In this section, we first introduce DPO and its application in diffu-
sion models in Sec. 3.1, followed by a detailed explanation of the
modeling process and training objectives of proposed SC-DPO in
Sec. 3.2. To support the training of SC-DPO, we present the safety
cost model in Sec. 3.3 and introduce the dynamic focusing mech-
anism employed in Sec. 3.4. Finally, we describe the construction
process of the dataset used for SC-DPO training in Sec. 3.5.

ptimization  Conference’17, July 2017, Washington, DC, USA
3.1  Preliminary
3.1.1  Direct Preference Optimization. DPO [31] is an effective
technique for aligning human preferences on Large language mod-
els. It reconstructs Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
(RLHF) [4, 44, 55] as a single-stage supervised learning problem,
eliminating the need for modeling display rewards.

parameterize 𝑅(𝑥, 𝑦) and use maximum likelihood for parameter
estimation. Treating this problem as a binary classification problem,
we can obtain a negative logarithmic likelihood function and derive
it into the following form through Bradley-Terry [2] modeling:

errors in predicting which output is preferred according to human
feedback. DPO [31] extends this framework by leveraging a critical
observation: when policy 𝜋 reaches its optimum under Kurdyka-
Lojasiewicz (KL) constraints, a strict mathematical relationship
exists between the optimal policy and the reward function. Based
on this insight, the original preference probability distribution in
Eq. (1) can be further derived as follows:

optimize for human preferences by learning to adjust its output
probabilities based on the feedback, thus aligning the model’s be-
havior more closely with human expectations and preferences.

the pioneering work being Diffusion-DPO [42]. Similarly, Diffusion-

3.2  Safety-Constraint DPO
As illustrated in Fig. 1 The core motivation of SC-DPO is to achieve
effective safety alignment in T2I diffusion models through the DPO
paradigm. Inspired by Diffusion-DPO [42], the proposed SC-DPO
framework aims to further incorporate safety constraints into the
preference alignment process, which helps to better balance the
trade-off between generating safer and higher-quality samples.
Specifically, we strive to consider both human preferences and
the safety of the sample pairs during training. To this end, we first
define the combined reward function for image samples as follows:
𝑅𝜆 (𝑇, 𝐼 ) = 𝑅(𝑇, 𝐼 ) − 𝜆 · 𝐶(𝐼 ),  (4)
where 𝑅(𝑇, 𝐼 ) represents the preference reward function for the
sample, which can be considered as the semantic alignment between
the generated image and the text prompt, as well as the quality
of the generated image. In practice, we use Pickscore [21], a pre-
trained T2I quality evaluation model, to implement 𝑅(𝑇, 𝐼 ). 𝐶(𝐼 )
represents the safety cost function of the sample, which reflects the
harmfulness of the generated image at the visual level. For 𝐶(𝐼 ), we
introduce a safety cost model trained on enormous harmful concept
samples, which will be detailed in Sec.

reflects the preference distribution under security constraints:

3.3  Safety Cost Modeling
To precise quantification of the safety of generated images while
avoiding complex manual annotation, we train a cost model on
a diversified safety-labeled dataset, transforming implicit safety
criteria into explicit numerical scores. The cost model needs to have
two functionalities: (1) label whether a pair of images is harmful,

and (2) when both images in the pair are labeled as safe, accurately
compare the potential level of harm present in the images.
To this end, we first collected 15,000 harmful concept prompts
from datasets like DiffusionDB [43] and I2P [38], then randomly
generated harmful images of varying degrees and concepts using
Stable Diffusion V1.5 [35] and SDXL [30]. By combining harmful
samples with each other and with randomly selected clean samples,
we created 80K image pairs as the raw image pair data. Each image
pair are further automatically defined as (𝐼𝑤, 𝐼𝑙 ) and annotated by
a binary harmful classification labels {𝑆𝑤, 𝑆𝑙 }, which are quantified
using the following symbolic function:

Specifically, we use GPT-4o to assess the harmfulness of the samples,
thereby distinguishing 𝐼𝑤 and 𝐼𝑙 , which represent the samples with
higher and lower harmfulness, respectively, in the image pair.
Inspired by the PickScore reward model [21], we use the Open-
CLIP (ViT/H-14) image encoder as the backbone and add a adapter
layer for fine-tuning to predict safety cost values, as illustrated in
Fig. 2,. We define the training objective of the safety cost model
𝐶(·) as the following contrastive learning loss form:
L𝐶𝑇𝑅𝑆 = E 𝐼  ,𝐼 ,𝑆  ,𝑆  [log𝜎 (𝐶(𝐼𝑤) −𝐶(𝐼𝑙 ))

where 𝜂 is a weight constant and we set to 100. Although training
with L𝐶𝑇𝑅𝑆 effectively quantifies the safety cost of paired images,
we observed that the reward score differences in Eq (4) are very
small when scoring image pairs with harmful classification labels
as "harmless". In this case, if the safety cost difference is too large, it
may cause the overall preference fit to overly emphasize potential
harm, deviating from normal human preferences. To address this
issue, we introduce the cost anchoring loss for the image pairs that
are both classified as "harmless":

4.1  Experimental Settings
Datasets. (1) As mentioned in Sec. 3.5, we utilized the constructed
SCP-10K dataset for the training of SC-DPO. It includes 7,000/3000

I2P-Sexual [38] and sexual subset from NSFW-56K [24] to conduct
a more comprehensive comparison of baseline methods. (3) To eval-
uate the human preferences of generated samples, we used datasets
including Pick-to-Pic [21], HPD [45], and DrawBench [37], and
assessed the image quality of generated samples using the COCO-
30K [23].In the experiments, we randomly selected 1,000 prompts
from the Pick-a-pic test set and 200 prompts from the DrawBench
dataset, generating one sample per prompt. Additionally, we used
400 text prompts from the HPD test set, generating two samples per
prompt. For the COCO-30K dataset, we randomly selected 3,000
prompts, generating one sample per prompt. All test images are
generated under a fixed set of random seeds.
Baselines. Following previous research, we applied the proposed
SC-DPO for safety alignment on Stable Diffusion v1.5 (SD-v1.5) [35],
which is the most widely used open-source T2I model. In addition to
comparing with the official weights, we conducted a comprehensive
comparison on SD-v1.5 with state-of-the-art T2I safety methods,
including filtering-based methods (SD-v1.5 w/ Safety Filter [5]),
concept erasure methods (SLD [38], ESD [9], UCE [10], RECE [12]
and SafetyDPO [25]). Specifically, for SLD, we used both Medium

Evaluation Metrics. To evaluate the effectiveness of safety align-
ment, we follow the setup in RECE [12], using NudeNet [26] for
detecting six exposed body parts: Breast (BR), Genitalia (GE), But-
tocks (BU), Feet (FE), Belly (BE), and Armpits (AR).

We report the
detection counts for each body part and the total number (Total) of
body parts detected across different methods. For broader harmful
concepts, we adopt the inappropriate probability (IP) metric used
in SLD [38] to measure the proportion of unsafe images generated
by T2I models under harmful prompts. To evaluate the human
preference alignment of generated images across various meth-
ods, we use multiple human preference scoring metrics, including
Pickscore [21], Hpsv2 [45], ImageReward [46], AES [39], and CLIP-
Score [16]. Additionally, to assess the image quality and instruction
adherence, we use FID [17] and LPIPS [50] metrics.
Implementation Details. We trained the SD-v1.5 model using the
AdamW optimizer within the SC-DPO framework. The training

UCE [10] and SafetyDPO [25]. Compared to other methods, SC
was conducted on 6 NVIDIA 4090D GPUs, with a batch size of 2
per GPU and a gradient accumulation step of 1, resulting in an
adequate batch size 12. The model was trained for 15,000 steps
across these six GPUs, with a learning rate of 8e-8 for each GPU
and a 500-step linear warmup. Each training session took approxi-
mately 6 hours to complete. The parameter 𝜆 is set to 0.15 given
the ablation analysis in Sec. 6. The data augmentation strategy em-
ployed in our training pipeline included several key enhancements:
image sharpening, colour jittering, latent space noise perturbation
with small-scale erasure, and frequency band energy compensation.
These augmentation techniques improve the model’s robustness
and generalization ability across diverse input data.
4.2  Primary Results and Analysis (R&A)
R&A-1: SC-DPO achieves state-of-the-art safety alignment
performance on sexual concepts, significantly reducing the
probability of generating sensitive body areas. Since sexuality is
the most typical NSFW concept, we first explored SC-DPO’s safety
alignment performance on harmful concepts related to sexuality.
Table 1 summarizes the inappropriate probability (IP) of various
safety alignment methods on I2P-Sexual andNSFW-56K. The results
show that SC-DPO has the lowest IP values on the I2P-Sexual and
NSFW-56K datasets, with values of 4.40% and 13.65%, respectively,
far lower than the 31.90% and 57.05% values of the original weights
of SD-v1.5, and surpassing RECE (4.70% and 13.70%) as well as the
recent similar work SafetyDPO (10.53% and 19.80%).
We further conducted a fine-grained statistic of exposed body
parts in the generated images, using NudeNet for detection. The
results in Table 2 show that SC-DPO outperforms in reducing the

Figure 5: Resistance Against Adversarial Attacks. We em-
ployed two advanced text-based T2I adversarial attacks,
SneakyPrompt [48] and MMA [47], and reported the inap-
propriate content probability under these attack methods.
The SC-DPO method achieves outstanding safety alignment perfor-
mance compared to existing baseline methods. When examining the
average inappropriate probability across all categories, SC-DPO’s
lowest score is 17.07%, significantly improved compared to the
second-best method SLD [38] with a score of 20.09%. Across all
NSFW categories, SC-DPO demonstrates significant effectiveness
in most harmful types. Specifically, SC-DPO achieves the best per-
formance in five out of the seven categories: Hate (9.52%), Violence
(17.2%), Self-harm (15.11%), Sex (14.61%), and Shocking Content
(11.33%). This broad effectiveness highlights SC-DPO’s ability to
learn comprehensive safety concepts rather than focusing on a sin-
gle or limited harmful content type. We also observed that the cate-
gory where SC-DPO performs relatively weaker is Illegal Content,
which may be due to the long-tail nature of the sample distribution
for illegal concepts in the SCP training dataset. Therefore, the tar-
geted introduction of more comprehensive preference data may be
required for improvement. In Fig. 4, we further visualize generated
images of different methods under different harmful prompts.
R&A-3: Compared to previous safety alignment methods, SC-
DPO achieves a better balance between sample generation qual-
ity and safety alignment performance. Since SC-DPO does not
sorely align safe preference samples, but integrates safety costs
into human preference alignment, it demonstrates significant ad-
vantages in human preference scores and instruction-following de-
gree. In Tab. 4, SC-DPO consistently outperforms baseline models
and competing safety alignment methods (SafetyDPO) on various
I2T benchmark. For instance, on the Pick-a-Pic dataset, SC-DPO
achieved the highest ImageReward score of 0.282, far surpassing
RECE (0.071) and SafetyDPO (-0.106). This indicates that our method
can generate images that are more aesthetically pleasing to humans
while maintaining safety constraints.
Tab. 5 further demonstrates the outstanding balance SC-DPO
achieves between safety alignment and generative capability, based
on sample quality and instruction adherence evaluations on the
COCO-30K dataset. It attained the highest CLIPScore (26.6, consis-
tent with the base SD-v1.5 model). At the same time, other safety
methods showed a decline in this metric (SLD-MEDIUM: 25.8, SLD-
STRONG: 25.0, SafetyDPO: 25.7). Additionally, SC-DPO achieved
the highest LPIPS score (0.447) and maintained a highly compet-
itive FID value (19.4), significantly outperforming methods like
SafetyDPO (22.3) and SLD-STRONG (21.3).

4.3  Ablation Studies and Additional Discussion
Impact of safety cost weight 𝜆. We conducted ablation experi-
ments with different safety cost weights and reported the changes in
SC-DPO’s IP value on the I2P dataset and CLIPScore on COCO-30K,
as shown in Tab. 6 When 𝜆 is lowered, the proportion of safety cost
in the combined reward function decreases, leading to a correspond-
ing decline in the model’s safety alignment performance. When 𝜆
is too high, the excessive emphasis on safety alignment leads to
a decrease in the sample quality reflected by the CLIPScore. An
intuitive understanding is that when 𝜆 increases to a certain level,
the preference-relabelled samples will fully reflect the safety pref-
erences, and SC-DPO will degrade to the form of SafetyDPO [25].
Therefore, a reasonable 𝜆 value helps balance sample quality and
safety alignment performance.
Robustness against adversarial attacks.

We further conducted
jailbreak attacks on different safety alignment methods using ad-
versarial harmful prompts constructed with SneakyPrompt [48]
and MMA [47]. Figure 5 shows the IP values of generated images
by different methods under this setup. From the results, we can see
that SC-DPO has the lowest IP values, with 5.5% for SneakyPrompt
and 30.2% for MMA, lower than previous safety alignment methods
such as UCE (12.0% and 36.8%) and the second-best method ESD-u
(9.0% and 33.8%). This demonstrates that SC-DPO has stronger resis-
tance to adversarial samples, further highlighting its effectiveness
and robustness in safety alignment.
Effectiveness on different T2I models. The proposed SC-DPO
is a scalable training framework suitable for different diffusion.

In this paper, we introduced Safety-Constrained Direct Preference
Optimization (SC-DPO), a novel framework for ensuring safety
alignment in Text-to-Image (T2I) generation models. SC-DPO aims
to effectively balance the need for safety with the preservation of
generation quality, addressing two key challenges in the T2I space:
the defence against harmful content and the retention of high-
quality generation. The extensive experimental results demonstrate
that SC-DPO outperforms existing methods, both in terms of safety
alignment and generation quality. Our work lays the foundation for
future research into more nuanced safety constraints in AI-driven
creative systems, promoting ethical standards in content creation.
